questions.questionText: Coup and contrecoup injuries are seen in:
questions.optionB: Head injuries — coup at the site of impact, contrecoup on the opposite side (brain deceleration against skull)
questions.explanation: Coup: brain injury at the SITE of impact (skull strikes the brain — moving object hits stationary head). Contrecoup: brain injury OPPOSITE to the site of impact (brain decelerates against skull — head in motion strikes stationary object, e.g., fall). Contrecoup usually more severe. Mechanism: brain lags behind skull movement → impacts opposite wall. Common in frontal and temporal lobes (bony irregularities of anterior cranial fossa). Subdural hematoma: bridging veins rupture (deceleration). Extradural: middle meningeal artery (temporal fracture). Reference: Reddy — Head Injuries.

questions.questionText: Battle's sign (bruising behind the ear over mastoid process) indicates:
questions.optionB: Fracture of the base of skull (posterior cranial fossa/petrous temporal bone fracture)
questions.explanation: Battle's sign: ecchymosis (bruising) behind the ear over mastoid process → indicates fracture of petrous temporal bone/posterior cranial fossa base of skull fracture. Appears 24-72 hours after injury. Other base of skull fracture signs: Raccoon eyes (periorbital ecchymosis — anterior fossa), CSF rhinorrhea (anterior), CSF otorrhea (middle fossa), hemotympanum. Ring sign: CSF and blood on filter paper — central blood stain with surrounding clear ring (halo). Reference: Reddy — Skull Fractures.
questions.conceptTag: Battle sign base of skull fracture

questions.questionText: A pugilistic attitude (boxer stance) of the body found in a fire victim indicates:
questions.optionB: Post-mortem heat coagulation of proteins in muscles (flexion posture — occurs even if victim was dead before fire)
questions.explanation: Pugilistic attitude (pugilistic stance/fencing posture): flexion posture of extremities (resembling a boxer) seen in burn victims. Caused by heat coagulation and shrinkage of muscle proteins (muscles shorten) — predominantly affects flexors (stronger than extensors). Occurs POST-MORTEM (does NOT indicate ante-mortem activity — can occur even if person was dead before fire). Cannot be used to determine if victim was alive or dead at time of fire. Heat fractures, heat hematoma (extradural — chocolate brown, not clotted) also occur post-mortem. Reference: Reddy — Burns Forensic.
